data.title: Burlington Stores: The Next Leg Of Upside Is From Earnings Growth
data.description:
Investment action

I reiterated my buy rating for Burlington Stores (BURL) previously, as I believed the SSSG weakness was not structural and that it would recover to mid-single-digits by Q4. What has changed so far is that SSSG rebounded to 4% in Q4 2025 despite a weather hit and a deliberate pullback in tariff-pressured categories, while margins still expanded nicely. The margin expansion story has also gotten better.

Reported sales understate how good demand really was

To start, I think it is important for readers to realize that BURL did much better than the headline numbers in the recent results update. Firstly, a 4% SSSG on top of 6% last year is already strong. But that could have been much better, as management said that BURL could have done more sales in Q4, especially in toys, gifting, housewares, bedding, and seasonal décor. The issue was not that customers were not buying. The issue was that BURL had already cut back its plans in businesses where tariffs had made the margin math less attractive. So this was a choice made to “grow less” to protect margins, not that demand was weak. In hindsight, it would have been great for BURL to capture those demands, as absolute earnings growth is more important than margins (in my opinion). Nonetheless, this tells us that the consumer demand backdrop is strong, and BURL could have printed low teens two-year stacked SSSG.

Another element to consider is the winter storm. Management noted that November and December ran at a mid-single-digit SSSG. January also ran at a mid-single-digit SSSG pace before the winter storm disrupted several hundred stores. The estimate provided by management was that the storm cost ~100 bps of SSSG for the Q4 quarter and several points for January. Then February reverted back on track (I assume this meant back to a mid-single-digit pace).

If we combine that self-imposed pullback to not capture lower-margin demand plus the weather disruption, I think it is safe to say that BURL would’ve printed a new recent high SSSG of potentially high single digits. The quality of this growth also matters. This was not driven by a single pocket of customers. Management specifically noted Q4 trends were broad-based across income cohorts and across ethnic trade areas. Lower-income areas actually did slightly better, but only slightly. This narrative does help to push back against the idea that BURL is only doing well because it benefits from the trade-down motion.

Margin expansion

The margin expansion story also looks much better now. I believe the margin expansion BURL achieved this past quarter was structural, and there should be more room to expand. Part of that came from the back-end initiatives such as better freight planning and lower shortages. But one of the more important initiatives is on the merchandise side, the elevation strategy. In simple terms, that means BURL has been moving the assortment up by offering better and more recognizable brands, higher quality, and more fashion, while still keeping the value proposition intact. This has worked really well, as BURL is seeing higher customer perception scores, stronger SSSG in the higher price buckets, a mid-single-digit increase in average unit retail, and also higher transaction size. Clearly, it is not that lower-income customers don’t want branded goods; they are willing to pay if the price is right, and BURL has surely done a right job pricing them.

Another margin expansion catalyst is localization. For context, this means buying and allocating with more precision, which also means there will be a lower percentage of inventories sitting on the shelf (not sold) and fewer markdowns later on. Management themselves have noted this as a major opportunity and admitted BURL is behind peers. We have to see how well BURL can execute here, but the preparations are mostly done, as BURL has built enough planning and allocation capability to start pushing harder here. To note a few, BURL now has better store planning, stronger localization analytics, and new store assortment planning and is anchoring assortment decisions to demographic ones. If this works, we should see continuous uplift of SSSG, which should flow down nicely to the margin line.

Valuation

As I expected, BURL’s SSSG accelerated back to mid-single-digits, and the stock reacted very positively. Back in November, the stock traded at the -1 standard deviation level (~24x NTM PE) but is now at ~28x NTM PE. The relative discount vs. peers has also closed to 4% (from 9%). From here, the upside is less multiple re-rating and more earnings, which is still attractive. With mid-single-digit SSSG existing in FY2025 and the number of stores open still growing at high-single-digits, we could easily bridge to low-to-mid-teen revenue growth. At that pace of growth, even with margins flat, adj. EPS could easily grow in the north of 10% (probably high teens since there would be some form of margin expansion from all the fixed costs).

Using consensus estimates (high-teens adj. EPS CAGR), BURL is expected to deliver ~$15.9 adj. EPS in FY28, and at 28x, that implies a share price target of $445.2.

Risk

BURL now has more moving parts to control within the business. It has the elevation strategy, the localization initiatives, and also the new Savannah distribution center to ramp up. If localization does not improve allocation as expected, or if the DC startup creates more disruption or cost than planned, the margin expansion may not play out. This could really weigh on the stock sentiment, especially with BURL now trading at 28x NTM PE.

Conclusion

I give BURL a buy rating. This quarter confirmed that BURL SSSG can return to mid-single-digit growth (and beyond). It also showed that underlying demand was stronger than reported and that the margin expansion story is getting more structural. The stock multiple is no longer as cheap as it was a few months ago, so I agree the next leg of upside is likely to come more from earnings than multiple rerating.
